misc.title: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 There’s a Japanese proverb that Connie, from the Sorbet Kingdom, also referred to: “能ある鷹は爪を隠す: A skilled hawk hides its claws.” It means that someone
misc.text: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 There’s a Japanese proverb that Connie, from the Sorbet Kingdom, also referred to: “能ある鷹は爪を隠す: A skilled hawk hides its claws.” It means that someone with real talent or intelligence doesn’t go around showing it off for no reason. That idea is perfectly embodied by "Hawk-Eye Mihawk". He’s not the kind of person who flaunts his strength on his own. That’s probably why he called Zoro “a weakling” at first—because Zoro was the one trying to prove himself by challenging him. And when you hear “爪 claws,” another thing that comes to mind is the Hoof of the Flying Dragon. That’s the symbol of the "Celestial Dragons". Hancock, for example, desperately wants no one to see that claw-shaped mark, because it’s the brand of a slave. So if Mihawk is someone who “hides his claws,” then maybe that also suggests he wants to hide the claws he himself carries. A hawk that hides its claws. In other words, it could even be a metaphor for Mihawk hiding the fact that he carries "Celestial Dragon" blood. Stussy Sama - Dawn&Dusk (@DawnStussy) x.com/i/article/204221152086… — https://nitter.net/DawnStussy/status/2042241260086612180#m

items.title: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 There’s a Japanese proverb that Connie, from the Sorbet Kingdom, also referred to: “能ある鷹は爪を隠す: A skilled hawk hides its claws.” It means that someone
items.text: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 There’s a Japanese proverb that Connie, from the Sorbet Kingdom, also referred to: “能ある鷹は爪を隠す: A skilled hawk hides its claws.” It means that someone with real talent or intelligence doesn’t go around showing it off for no reason. That idea is perfectly embodied by "Hawk-Eye Mihawk". He’s not the kind of person who flaunts his strength on his own. That’s probably why he called Zoro “a weakling” at first—because Zoro was the one trying to prove himself by challenging him. And when you hear “爪 claws,” another thing that comes to mind is the Hoof of the Flying Dragon. That’s the symbol of the "Celestial Dragons". Hancock, for example, desperately wants no one to see that claw-shaped mark, because it’s the brand of a slave. So if Mihawk is someone who “hides his claws,” then maybe that also suggests he wants to hide the claws he himself carries. A hawk that hides its claws. In other words, it could even be a metaphor for Mihawk hiding the fact that he carries "Celestial Dragon" blood. Stussy Sama - Dawn&Dusk (@DawnStussy) x.com/i/article/204221152086… — https://nitter.net/DawnStussy/status/2042241260086612180#m
